question: MDP framework: state the components and Bellman equation.

options: Markov Decision Process: $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$ with states $S$, actions $A$, transition $P(s'|s,a)$, reward $R(s,a)$, discount $\gamma \in [0,1)$. Bellman optimality: $V^*(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma\sum_{s'}P(s'|s,a)V^*(s')]$. Policy $\pi(a|s)$; value functions $V^\pi, Q^\pi$; goal: maximise expected discounted return
MDPs have only one state and one action (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (not supported by published research or production deployments)
No reward needed; pure exploration (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)
Bellman equation is for value functions only (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations)
passive.explanation: Markov property: future depends only on current state, not history. Discounted return: $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r_{t+k}$. Policy $\pi$: $\pi(a|s)$ probability of action $a$ in state $s$. Value functions: $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb E_\pi[G_t | s_t = s]$, $Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb E_\pi[G_t | s_t = s, a_t = a]$. Bellman expectation: $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb E_{a\sim\pi}[Q^\pi(s,a)]$. Optimal value: $V^*(s) = \max_\pi V^\pi(s)$. Bellman optimality (Bellman 1957): recursive equation, foundation of dynamic programming. Solution methods: value iteration, policy iteration (exact in tabular, finite-state). For large/continuous: function approximation (deep RL).

active.prompt: Match each RL algorithm family with its approach:

pairs.definition: Learn $Q(s,a)$; act greedily/$\epsilon$-greedy; foundational, sample-efficient in discrete actions
Directly parametrise $\pi_\theta$; ascend $\nabla_\theta J$; works with continuous actions
Combines value (critic) + policy (actor); variance reduction; modern default for many tasks
Learn world model, plan in imagination; sample-efficient; complex but very strong on Atari/Go

active.explanation: RL algorithm taxonomy: model-free (Q-learning, policy gradient) vs model-based (learn $P, R$). On-policy (PPO) vs off-policy (DQN, SAC, replay buffer). Discrete (DQN, Rainbow) vs continuous actions (DDPG, TD3, SAC). Modern apps: game AI (AlphaGo, AlphaStar), robotics, LLM alignment (RLHF), recommendation systems, finance.

question: Q-learning: how does it work and why off-policy?

options: Iterative update: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$. Off-policy: target uses $\max_{a'}$ — optimal action — regardless of actual action taken in collection; behaviour policy can be anything explorative (e.g.\ $\epsilon$-greedy). Provably converges in tabular setting; with function approx (DQN) requires tricks (replay, target net)

Q-learning is on-policy (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses)

Updates use current policy's action, not max (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)

Cannot learn optimal Q-function (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)
passive.explanation: Q-learning (Watkins 1989): foundational off-policy TD learning. Update: TD error $\delta_t = r_t + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$. Off-policy: bootstrap target uses greedy (optimal w.r.t.\ current $Q$) action regardless of what was actually taken — allows learning from arbitrary behaviour policy. Tabular convergence: under all-state-action visited infinitely often + learning rate decay. With NN function approximation: instabilities. DQN (Mnih et al.\ 2015, Nature): (i) Experience replay — i.i.d.\ training samples; (ii) Target network — fixed copy of $Q$ for bootstrap target, updated periodically. Achieved human-level Atari from raw pixels. Rainbow (2018): combines double Q, prioritised replay, dueling, noisy nets, distributional, multi-step. SAC, TD3 continuous extensions.

question: Policy gradient theorem: state the result and derive REINFORCE.

options: $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb E_{\tau\sim\pi_\theta}[\sum_t \nabla_\theta\log\pi_\theta(a_t|s_t) Q^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t)]$ — gradient through stochastic policy; REINFORCE replaces $Q$ with empirical return $G_t$; high variance. Variance reduction: subtract baseline (state value $V$); actor-critic uses learned critic
Cannot differentiate through sampling (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)

Policy gradient requires Hessian (not supported by published research or production deployments) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses)
Theorem applies only to deterministic policies (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))
passive.explanation:
Policy gradient theorem (Sutton 2000): elegant identity allowing optimisation of $J(	heta) = \mathbb E_{s_0,a_0,\ldots\sim\pi_	heta}[\sum\gamma^t r_t]$ without differentiating through environment. Use likelihood ratio (score function) estimator: $
abla\mathbb E_{x\sim p_	heta}[f(x)] = \mathbb E[f(x)
abla\log p_	heta(x)]$. Result: gradient is $\mathbb E[
abla\log\pi_	heta(a|s) Q^\pi(s,a)]$. REINFORCE (Williams 1992): $\hat
abla = \sum_t G_t 
abla\log\pi_	heta(a_t|s_t)$ — unbiased but high variance. Variance reduction: subtract baseline $b(s)$, doesn't change expectation: $
abla\log\pi_	heta\cdot(G - b)$. Optimal baseline = $V^\pi(s)$. Actor-critic methods: $A^\pi(s,a) = Q - V$ advantage; learned critic; A2C/A3C, PPO most popular. PPO (Schulman 2017): clipped surrogate objective $\min(r\hat A, 	ext{clip}(r,1-\epsilon,1+\epsilon)\hat A)$ — stable, simple, default for RLHF.

question: Exploration vs exploitation: classical strategies and modern approaches.

options: $\epsilon$-greedy (random with prob $\epsilon$); Boltzmann/softmax action selection; UCB (upper confidence bound): exploration bonus $\sim\sqrt{\log t/n_a}$; Thompson sampling: sample from posterior over rewards. Deep RL: NoisyNet, curiosity-driven (intrinsic reward $\propto$ prediction error of dynamics model), RND, count-based exploration
Pure exploitation always optimal (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)

Random forever; no exploitation (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)
Trade-off is solved by neural networks automatically (not supported by published research or production deployments) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses)
passive.explanation: Classical bandit theory: $\epsilon$-greedy simple but suboptimal regret (linear in $\epsilon$). UCB (Auer 2002): pick $\arg\max[\hat r_a + c\sqrt{\log t/n_a}]$ — optimism in face of uncertainty, $O(\log T)$ regret. Thompson sampling: sample reward params from posterior, act greedily w.r.t.\ sample. Both achieve optimal regret for bandits. For full MDPs: harder, exploration must navigate. Methods in deep RL: (1) Curiosity (Pathak 2017): intrinsic reward = $\|\hat\phi(s_{t+1}) - \phi(s_{t+1})\|^2$ for forward-dynamics model; explore unpredictable states. (2) RND (Random Network Distillation): exploration bonus = $\|\hat f(s) - f(s)\|$ where $f$ random network — solves Montezuma's Revenge sparse-reward. (3) Go-Explore (Ecoffet 2019): archive of visited states for revisiting. (4) Maximum entropy RL (SAC): policy entropy bonus encourages exploration.

question: AlphaGo and successors: what's the algorithmic recipe?

options: MCTS (Monte Carlo Tree Search) guided by neural network value + policy. AlphaGo (2016): policy net trained on human games + value net + MCTS; beat Lee Sedol. AlphaZero (2017): pure self-play, no human data; chess, shogi, Go. MuZero (2019): learns transition model in latent space; works without rules access. AlphaStar (StarCraft), AlphaFold (proteins) — same general approach

Pure deep learning, no search (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)

Random play matches AlphaGo (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))

AlphaGo used only handcrafted features (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (not supported by published research or production deployments) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)
passive.explanation: AlphaGo (Silver 2016): combine deep RL with classical game-tree search. Policy network $p_\sigma(a|s)$: trained via supervised learning on $\sim 30M$ human moves, then refined via self-play policy gradient. Value network $v_\theta(s)$: predicts game outcome from state, trained on self-play games. Inference: MCTS with $p_\sigma$ for action priors, $v_\theta$ for leaf evaluation, rollouts for additional signal. Beat Lee Sedol 4-1 (March 2016). AlphaGo Zero (2017): pure self-play tabula rasa, no human games; trained 3 days, exceeded all prior versions. AlphaZero (Dec 2017): single algorithm handles Go, chess, shogi; matched/exceeded best engines (Stockfish, Elmo). MuZero (2019): learns environment model in latent space → applies same approach to Atari + classic games. AlphaTensor (2022): matrix multiplication algorithms. AlphaFold (2020-21): protein structure prediction, RL-inspired but mostly supervised.

question: Offline RL: what's the challenge and what techniques are emerging?

options: Learn from fixed dataset of (s,a,r,s') tuples — no further interaction. Challenge: distribution shift between behaviour policy and learned policy; bootstrapping error compounds. Techniques: constrain policy near behaviour (BCQ, BEAR); pessimism on out-of-distribution actions (CQL); offline-RL→online finetune. Applications: robotics with safety constraints, medical, large-scale recommendations

Offline RL is identical to imitation learning (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations)

Cannot learn from fixed datasets (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)

Always better than online RL (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (not supported by published research or production deployments)
passive.explanation: Offline (batch) RL: no environment interaction, learn purely from logged data. Critical: many applications can't afford online exploration (medical, robotics, recommendations). Naive Q-learning fails: out-of-distribution action queries return wildly optimistic Q values, policy moves there, gets bad. Approaches: (1) Behaviour-constrained: BCQ (Fujimoto 2019) restricts to actions near data; BEAR uses MMD; (2) Conservative: CQL (Kumar 2020) — penalty pushes down OOD Q-values; (3) Implicit Q-learning (IQL, Kostrikov 2021): avoid sampling OOD actions via expectile regression; (4) Decision Transformer (Chen 2021): cast RL as sequence modelling, condition on returns. Use case explosion: D4RL benchmarks (robotic manipulation, locomotion, mazes); industrial recommendation (Netflix, YouTube), robotics demonstrations (Tesla, Boston Dynamics). LLM RLHF largely offline / batch RL on preference data.
